steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make it a past tense verb
Added alif after first letter and changed vowels to make it describe someone who does the action
Added ات to make it feminine plural

steps.m: the ones (feminine) who pull out forcefully
steps.n: Added ال with shadda on following letter to make it definite

steps.m: and the ones (feminine) who pull out forcefully
steps.n: Added و meaning 'and' at start, and changed ending to match oath pattern

steps.m: to pull out or extract forcefully

steps.n: Changes from verb 'to drown' into the noun 'drowning'

steps.m: with a forceful pulling out (describes how النَّازِعٰتِ pull out)
steps.n: The ًا ending makes this word describe how the action is done. It's connected to النَّازِعٰتِ (those who pull out) - explaining that they do their pulling in a forceful way.
Added fathas to connect the root letters into a past tense verb
Adding alif after first root letter makes it describe who does the action. This word is the subject (doer) of the action نشطا in the ayah.
Adding ات makes it feminine plural

steps.m: the ones (feminine) who pull out vigorously
steps.n: Adding definite article makes it definite

steps.m: and the ones (feminine) who pull out vigorously (referring to angels drawing forth souls)
steps.n: Added conjunction وَ and kasra ending. In this Surah, the word refers to angels who draw forth souls.
The root letters are given a pattern (نَفْع) to make it mean 'the act of pulling' instead of 'to pull'. It describes how the angels do their job.

steps.m: with vigorous pulling (describing how the angels work)
steps.n: Adding the -an ending (tanween) changes this from a noun into a word that describes how something is done

steps.m: to glide smoothly, to move swiftly

steps.n: Changes from verb (to swim) to noun (swimmer)

steps.m: those who glide very swiftly (feminine plural)
steps.n: Adds feminine plural ending to show multiple female entities. This word is the subject doing the swimming/gliding action mentioned in سَبْحًا.

steps.m: and the ones who glide very swiftly
steps.n: Added 'wa' (and) at start + 'al' (the) to connect with previous phrase
Removes the 'and' (وَ) at the start and adds a mark ( ِ ) at the end to show it belongs to something
Changes verb into a noun describing the action itself. This word describes how the السابحات (those that glide) move.
Adds ًا to show this describes how something is done. This word explains how السَّابِحٰتِ (the gliders) move.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Added alif after first letter and kasra under second letter to show who does the action
Added ات to make it feminine plural, referring to multiple female entities

steps.m: the female ones who race ahead
steps.n: Added ال at the beginning to make it specific and doubled the س with shadda

steps.m: so the female ones who race ahead (referring to angels racing to carry out Allah's commands)
steps.n: Added فَ (meaning 'so/then') to connect to previous verse, and changed the ending slightly for grammar
Changed the root into a noun that names the action itself. This word describes what type of action the angels (السَّابِقٰتِ) are doing.
The ًا ending shows this word describes how the angels are racing ahead
Adding مُ at beginning shows who does the action, while َ and ِ connect the letters
Double letter shows doing something a lot or with strength
Adding ات shows we're talking about multiple feminine doers
Added ال to show we mean specific managers, not just any managers

steps.m: so/then the multiple managers (feminine) (who manage أَمْرًا - the matter)
steps.n: Added فَ to connect this to what came before. These managers are doing the managing of 'the matter' (أَمْرًا) mentioned after this word.
Added vowel marks to make the root into a noun meaning 'command' or 'matter'. This word is what is being arranged/managed by الْمُدَبِّرٰتِ (those who arrange affairs).
Added tanween alif (ًا) to show this is an indefinite object - something being arranged/managed by الْمُدَبِّرٰتِ (those who arrange affairs).
Connected the letters together with vowel marks to make it a noun. The middle letter و stays as a consonant.
Added a fatha mark at the end to show this word is in the accusative case (used as an object)
Added fathas to connect the root letters and make it a past tense verb
Changed to present tense by adding تَ and changing vowels to damma
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Changed to active participle form to show who/what does the shaking
Added ة to make it refer to the event itself rather than the doer
Adding ال makes it specific/definite - referring to a particular quake
Adding ُ shows this word is the subject of the sentence
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense verb
Added تَ at start to change from past to present tense, and ُ at end to show it's doing the action
Added ها at the end to show that something feminine (the Raadifah) is being followed. This word appears in the phrase 'تَتْبَعُهَا ٱلرَّادِفَةُ' meaning 'the Following One follows it'.
Connected the root letters with fatha to create past tense verb
Added alif after first root letter and kasra under second letter to show who does the action
Added ة to make it feminine, since this refers to the second blast of the trumpet which follows the first
Added ال to make it specific and doubled the ر with shadda because it comes after ال
Changed alif to wasl form and added damma (ُ) to show it's doing the following action
The root meaning of turning/changing becomes 'heart' since the heart is seen as something that constantly changes states and turns emotions
Changes the word to show multiple hearts. These hearts are described as وَاجِفَةٌ (trembling/throbbing with fear) in this ayah
Connected the letters together with vowel marks to make the basic noun 'day'
Added fatha at the end to show this word connects to what comes after it

steps.m: on that day (when hearts will be trembling)
steps.n: Added ئِذٍ to mean 'that' or 'at that time'. This refers to the Day of Judgment being discussed in this part of the Quran, when hearts will be trembling (وَاجِفَةٌ)

steps.m: to throb, palpitate, or tremble quickly

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic past tense verb
Changes the verb into an active participle (something/someone doing the action). This describes how the hearts are in a state of trembling.
Added the feminine marker ة because قُلُوبٌ (hearts) is feminine in Arabic
Added tanween damma to show this is nominative (like قُلُوبٌ) and indefinite (not specific hearts)

steps.m: to see or perceive with eyes

steps.n: Changed from root letters to a noun meaning 'sight' by adding the vowel marks a-a
Changed from single 'sight' to plural 'eyes' by adding alif at start and lengthening middle
Added damma to show this is the subject of the sentence
The alif (ا) changed to a long alif (ٰ) - the meaning stays the same
Added feminine possessive pronoun. This word is the subject being described as خٰشِعَةٌ (cast down/humbled)
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the past tense verb
Changed middle vowel to kasra to show this is something that happens to the person rather than something they do
Added the feminine marker ة because it's describing أَبْصٰرُهَا (eyes) which is feminine in Arabic
Added tanween dammah to show this is indefinite and in the nominative case as it's describing the subject (أَبْصٰرُهَا)
Connected the root letters with fatha marks. The middle weak letter و changes to ا in past tense.
Adding يَ at the start shows present tense. The middle letter changes back to و with a dhamma when making present tense.

steps.m: they say (referring to the disbelievers questioning resurrection)
steps.n: Adding ونَ shows that many people are doing the action. In this ayah, it refers to the disbelievers who are questioning if they will be resurrected.
This is the base word that adds emphasis to a statement
Added نا at the end to show this applies to 'we'

steps.m: is it true that we...? (questioning what يَقُولُونَ/they say)
steps.n: Added أَ at the beginning with a hamza after it to turn this into a question expressing disbelief or surprise. This is connected to يَقُولُونَ (they say) in the ayah.
Added vowels and doubled the middle letter (marked with شدة) to make it mean 'he returned'
Changed to show someone/something receiving the action instead of doing it
Made it plural to show it's talking about many people

steps.m: surely we will be returned (in response to أَءِنَّا - 'will we')
steps.n: Added لَ to emphasize that this will definitely happen. This is answering the question 'will we' (أَءِنَّا) from earlier in the verse.

steps.m: in (connects to ٱلْحَافِرَةِ - the first state)
steps.n: This is a basic preposition that shows location or position. In this ayah, it connects to the word ٱلْحَافِرَةِ to show where they will be returned to.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Added alif after first letter and kasra under second letter to show who does the action
Added ة to create a noun meaning 'original state' - this is a metaphorical meaning where the first digging becomes the starting point or original state
Added ال to make it specific

steps.m: in the original state (in response to يَقُولُونَ - they say)
steps.n: Added kasra at the end because it follows فِى (in) from the ayah
This is the basic conditional/temporal particle meaning 'when'

steps.m: is it when? (expressing disbelief about عِظٰمًا نَّخِرَةً - decayed bones)
steps.n: Added the interrogative hamza (أَ) with an extra hamza (ء) to show emphasis and disbelief. In this ayah, it's expressing strong skepticism about resurrection after becoming decayed bones.
Changed the root letters to make 'he was' by adding fathas (the small diagonal lines above)
Command form ('Be!') of the word meaning 'was'. Like saying 'exist!' or 'happen!'
Added نَا (we) to كُنْ (was) to make 'we were'. The shadda shows the ن merged together. Used in a question about resurrection.
Root meaning 'big/great' becomes a concrete noun 'bone' - a basic noun pattern for body parts
Makes the singular 'bone' into a broken plural 'bones' by adding an alif
The word 'bones' gets a special mark (ـًا) at the end to show it's receiving the action. It's like asking 'Will we turn into bones?'
Added vowels to the root letters to make a word describing something decayed
Added ة to match with عِظٰمًا (bones) which is feminine
Added tanween (two fathas) to make the word indefinite
Added emphasis (shadda) to show bones that are totally decayed and crumbling
Adding vowel marks to make basic past tense verb
Changed middle letter و to ا and added وا at the end to show many people (they) did the action
This is the base form of the demonstrative pronoun used to point to something far away
تِلْ becomes تِلْكَ by adding كَ to make it mean 'that' for feminine things. Here it points to كَرَّةٌ (return/coming back to life) later in the sentence
This is the base form meaning 'when' or 'at that time'

steps.m: in that case/therefore (responding to قَالُوا - they said)
steps.n: Adding tanween alif (ًا) changes the meaning from just 'when' to show a result or consequence of what was just said. In this ayah, it's showing the consequence of what 'they said'.
Doubling the middle letter adds intensity to the meaning
Added ة to make it into a noun for one instance of the action
Added tanween dammah to make it indefinite (a return, not the return)

steps.m: to suffer loss or be at a disadvantage

steps.n: Adding vowels to show this is a past tense verb meaning 'to lose'
Changes verb to active participle (doer of action). This describes كَرَّةٌ (return) in the ayah.
Makes the word feminine to match كَرَّةٌ which is feminine
Adds the -un ending to show it's indefinite (not specific) and is describing كَرَّةٌ
This is the base emphatic particle that adds certainty to what follows
Added فَ at the beginning to show this follows from what came before
Added مَا at the end which restricts the meaning to 'only' or 'merely'
This is the base part of the feminine pronoun that shows we're talking about something feminine
Adding ىَ makes it a complete independent pronoun that can be used as a subject. In this ayah, it's the subject that refers back to the resurrection mentioned earlier.

steps.m: to shout/cry out (especially as a rebuke or warning)

steps.n: Changed from basic root to a noun meaning 'a single shout/cry' by adding vowels

steps.m: a single shout/cry (that will resurrect everyone)
steps.n: The ة shows this is one specific instance, and the ٌ shows it's indefinite (like 'a' in English). This refers to the single blast of the trumpet that will resurrect everyone for judgment.
Root letters connected with vowels to make an adjective meaning 'single/one'
Added alif after و to make the word mean 'one who is single/alone'
Added ة to make it feminine since it describes زَجْرَةٌ (cry/blast) which is feminine
Added tanween (double damma) to make it indefinite
This is a time word that shows when something happens. In this ayah, it introduces what happens at a specific time - when they will be on the awakened earth.

steps.m: and then when (followed by هُم بِٱلسَّاهِرَةِ)
steps.n: The فَ at the beginning shows this happens after something else mentioned before. It's like saying 'then' or 'so' in English to show one thing follows another.
This is the basic form that refers to a single male or thing

steps.m: they (referring to those who will be resurrected)
steps.n: Adding م changes it from singular to plural, showing we're talking about a group. In this ayah, it refers to those who will be resurrected.
The root relates to being awake or alert
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
Changes from 'he stayed awake' to 'someone who stays awake' by switching the middle letter to alif - makes it describe a person
The feminine form here is used metaphorically to name a type of land that seems endless and keeps one alert/awake
Adding ال makes it refer to a specific plain
The بِ means 'on/in' and the ِ ending shows this word follows another word
This is a question particle that always comes at the start of a sentence to turn it into a yes/no question
Added vowel marks to show exact pronunciation. This word turns the statement 'the story of Musa reached you' into the question 'Has the story of Musa reached you?'
Added small alif above the ى to help with pronunciation

steps.m: he came to you (question in هَلْ أَتَىٰكَ حَدِيثُ مُوسَىٰٓ - Has the story of Moses come to you?)
steps.n: Added كَ at the end to show the action was directed to 'you'. This word is part of a question starting with هَلْ (has/did).
Changes from verb 'to tell' into noun 'a narrative/speech'
The ُ shows this word is the subject of أَتَىٰكَ (has come to you)
This is a proper noun (name) borrowed from Hebrew. It doesn't have an Arabic root. The basic form is just the consonants.
Added the basic vowel marks to show how to pronounce the name
Added the alif maqsura (ى) with an overextension mark (ٰ) at the end, which is common in Arabic names borrowed from other languages
Added a small alif (ٓ) at the end to show this is part of a longer phrase. Here, Moses is being discussed in the 'story/narrative' (حَدِيثُ)

steps.m: when (introducing the time when his Lord called him)
steps.n: This is a time particle that connects to what happens next in the story. It specifically points to a moment in the past, like saying 'at that time when...'
The root letters show the basic meaning of calling out
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make a basic past tense verb
Adding alif after first root letter makes the action more emphatic
The alif maqsurah (ى with small alif above) is added because this is a weak-ending verb

steps.m: he called him (object of رَبُّهُ/his Lord)
steps.n: The هُ (hu) was added at the end to show who was being called - it means 'him'. So it changed from just 'called out' to 'called him'

steps.m: to care for and help grow
steps.n: The root conveys the idea of nurturing and caring
Connected the letters and added shadda to show this is someone who really takes care of others
Added damma to show this is the doer of the action in نَادَىٰهُ (called him)
Added هُۥ at the end to show this is his Lord. This word is doing the action in نَادَىٰهُ (called him)
The root letters show the basic idea of water moving through an area
Changed the verb into a noun for a place where the action happens
Added ال to make it specific (the valley) and removed the tanween

steps.m: in the valley (where Allah spoke to Moses)
steps.n: Added ب meaning 'in' at the beginning. This valley is where Allah spoke to Moses, as mentioned in the ayah.
The root relates to purity and holiness
Forms a word describing something that has been made holy
Added 'the' to specify this particular holy valley being mentioned
Added kasra at the end to show this word describes the valley (وَادِ) mentioned before it
This is the proper name of the valley where Moses received revelation
Changes the word into the proper name of the sacred valley
Adding tanween (double vowel mark) to show this is a proper name being mentioned without 'the'
Added fathas to connect the letters and show past tense meaning
Changed from 'he went' to 'go!' by adding i- at the start and making the word shorter and choppier with sukun marks
The alif at the start helps pronounce the command form
Basic preposition showing direction or destination. In this ayah, it connects 'go' with 'Pharaoh' to mean 'go to Pharaoh'
Added vowel marks to show exact pronunciation: kasra (ِ) under إ
Added alif khanjariyah (ىٰ) at the end to show the elongated sound
This is a proper noun for the ruler of ancient Egypt. The vowel marks help with pronunciation.

steps.m: Pharaoh (object of اذْهَبْ إِلَىٰ - go to)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end to show Pharaoh is being acted upon - he is who the command 'go to' is directed towards
This is an emphasis particle that makes statements stronger. It's like adding 'truly' or 'definitely' at the start of a sentence.
Adding هُ shows who the word is talking about (he/him). In this ayah, this 'he' is doing the action in طَغَىٰ (transgressed).
The ۥ mark is just a writing helper that shows the هُ is pronounced clearly. It doesn't change the meaning.
Connected the letters with fatha to show this happened in the past
The final ي changes to ى with an alif above it (ٰ) because it's at the end of the word. This is a common spelling change that doesn't affect the meaning.
Changed to a command form by adding ُ on first letter and ْ on second letter. The middle letter و drops out because it's a weak letter.

steps.m: so say! (introducing what to say to فِرْعَوْنَ)
steps.n: Added فَ at the beginning to mean 'so' or 'then', showing this is what should happen next when speaking to فِرْعَوْنَ (Pharaoh)
This is a question word that starts yes/no questions. In this ayah, it's being used to politely ask Pharaoh if he would like something.
Added fatha mark to help with pronunciation

steps.m: surely, for/to (connected to فَقُلْ - then say)
steps.n: Adding shadda emphasizes the meaning

steps.m: surely for you (connected to فَقُلْ - then say)
steps.n: The كَ at the end shows this is directed to one male person
Basic preposition showing direction or movement towards something. Here it connects to the verb تَزَكَّىٰ (purify yourself)
Added alif maksura (ىٰ) at the end which is just a writing variation of alif - it doesn't change the meaning
Added a small alif (ٓ) above the word which helps with pronunciation but doesn't change the meaning
This is a connecting word that joins two parts of the sentence, introducing what someone is being asked to do. Here it connects to the verb تَزَكَّىٰ (purify yourself).
Added vowels and doubled the middle letter to show strong action. The و at the end changes to ى because it's a weak letter at the end.
Added تَ at the beginning to show doing the action to yourself
Added alif maksura (ىٰ) at the end to show this is the complete form

steps.m: to guide or show the way

steps.n: Changed from basic root to past tense 'he' form by adding vowels. The ي becomes ى at the end.
Changes from past tense 'he guided' to a command meaning 'you guide!'
Added كَ at the end to show you are receiving the guidance

steps.m: and I guide you (to رَبِّكَ your Lord)
steps.n: Added وَ at start to connect this word to what came before. This guidance is specifically towards 'your Lord' (رَبِّكَ) as shown in the next word.
This is a basic preposition that shows direction or movement towards something
Added ى at the end to complete the basic form of the preposition. This connects 'and guide you' with 'your Lord' to show where the guidance leads.
Added small alif (ٰ) above the ى to show it's pronounced as a long 'aa' sound
Added fatha on first letter and shadda on second letter to create the noun meaning 'lord'
Added ِكَ ('your') at the end to show it belongs to you. The kasra before ك shows it follows the word 'to' (إِلَىٰ)
Added vowels to connect the root letters and make a past tense verb
Added ت at beginning to show 'you' as the doer, and changed vowels for present tense. The ى at the end is because this is a weak verb ending in ي.

steps.m: so that you fear/are in awe (result of أَهْدِيَكَ - guide you)
steps.n: Added فَ to show this is the intended result of the guidance mentioned in أَهْدِيَكَ (guide you). The alif at the end (ىٰ) shows this is the end of the word.
Added basic vowels to connect the root letters
Form IV makes the verb causative - instead of 'to see', it becomes 'to make someone see/to show'. The final ي becomes ى due to weak letter rules.

steps.m: and then he showed (connected to previous action)
steps.n: فَ connects this to the previous action, showing sequence

steps.m: and then he showed him (the great sign)
steps.n: The هُ means 'him' - it shows who was shown something. This connects to the next words (ٱلْءَايَةَ ٱلْكُبْرَىٰ) which tell us what was shown - 'the great sign'
Connected the root letters with vowels. The first ي changes to ا because it's a weak letter.
Added ة to make it a specific instance of a sign/miracle

steps.m: the sign, the miracle (that Musa عليه السلام showed to Pharaoh)
steps.n: Added ٱلْ at start to make it specific and fatha at end to show it's the object of أَرَىٰهُ (he showed him)
Added vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Changed from a verb (became big) to a feminine superlative adjective (greatest) by adding ى and changing the vowels
Added ٱلْ (al) at the beginning to make it specific
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make it a past tense verb
Adding shadda (doubling mark) on middle letter makes the meaning stronger

steps.m: and so he denied (followed by وَعَصَىٰ - and disobeyed)
steps.n: Added فَ at the beginning to show this denial happened as a result of something before it. This word is followed by وَعَصَىٰ (and disobeyed) showing a sequence of rebellious actions.

steps.m: he disobeyed (connected to فَكَذَّبَ - and he denied)
steps.n: Added fathas and changed ي to ى because it's at the end. This shows the action happened in the past. This action follows after 'he denied' in the ayah.
Added وَ (and) at the beginning to connect this action with the previous action (denying)
Added small alif (ٰ) above the final ى to show the long 'aa' sound clearly. This doesn't change the meaning.
This is a common connecting word that links events in order. The shadda (doubled م) makes the word stronger.

steps.m: then (followed by أَدْبَرَ يَسْعَىٰ - he turned away, rushing)
steps.n: Added damma (ُ) and shadda with fatha (َّ) to show this is a strong connecting word. In this ayah, it connects to the next action where someone turned away rushing.
The root letters show the basic idea of moving backward or being behind something
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make a past tense verb
Adding أ at start makes the verb mean 'to do the action deliberately'
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make past tense verb
Added يَ at start to show present tense. The final ي changed to ى (alif maqsura) because it's at the end of the word.
Added dagger alif (ٰ) above the alif maqsura to show proper pronunciation

steps.m: to gather or collect things/people together
he gathered (followed by فَنَادَىٰ - and he called out)
steps.n: Added fathas to make it a past tense verb. This action happened before he called out (فَنَادَىٰ).

steps.m: and then he gathered (followed by فَنَادَىٰ - and he called out)
steps.n: The فَ prefix shows this gathering happened right after something else and right before he called out
Added fatha vowels and changed the final ي to ى to show past tense action
Added فَ (fa) to connect this action to what happened before

steps.m: he said (subject of أَنَا۠ رَبُّكُمُ ٱلْأَعْلَىٰ)
steps.n: Added fatha marks to show this happened in the past. The و changes to ا in the middle because it's a special letter. This word is connected to what comes next - the actual words he said: 'I am your highest lord'

steps.m: and then he said (subject of أَنَا۠ رَبُّكُمُ ٱلْأَعْلَىٰ)
steps.n: Added فَ at the beginning to show this happened after something else. This connects the story by showing one action followed another.
This is the basic form of the personal pronoun meaning 'I'. It doesn't come from a root since it's a fundamental pronoun.
Added an 'a' sound at the end to make it a stronger way to say 'I'
Added a special mark (۠) that shows this is being used in an emphasized way. In this ayah, Pharaoh is using this pronoun arrogantly to claim he is the highest lord.
Doubling the ب makes it stronger - shows complete mastery/lordship

steps.m: your (all) Lord (subject of قَالَ - he said)
steps.n: The suffix كُمُ shows this refers to multiple people being addressed. This word is the subject of قَالَ (he said) in this ayah, where Pharaoh is claiming to be their highest lord.
Added vowel marks to connect the letters and show past tense. The و changes to ا at the end because it's a weak letter.
Added أ at beginning and changed vowels to make it mean 'highest'. The ى at the end is because it's a special type of word (superlative).

steps.m: the highest (Pharaoh claiming to be higher than Allah)
steps.n: Added ال to make it specific. In this ayah, Pharaoh is arrogantly claiming to be the highest lord, above Allah.

steps.m: to grasp or take hold of

steps.n: Added fathas to connect the letters and show this happened in the past

steps.m: and then he took/seized (following what happened before)
steps.n: Added فَ at the beginning to show this action happened as a result of or following something else
Added هُ at the end to show who was seized. In this ayah, Allah (ٱللَّهُ) is doing the seizing, and Pharaoh is being seized.
Connected the letters and added vowels to make the word for 'deity'
Changed from the general word for 'deity' to become Allah (the proper name of God) by adding ال and a mark on top to show He is doing the action

steps.m: to punish, to make an example of

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make a past tense verb
Added alif after second root letter to transform the verb into a noun meaning the result of the punishment. This word is what Allah took/seized in the ayah.
Root meaning 'after/delayed' becomes 'last/final' by adding alif and vowels - like changing 'delay' to 'delayed'
Added feminine ending to create specific noun for 'the last life'
Added definite article to specify 'the' hereafter
Added kasra at the end to show this word is possessed by نَكَالَ (punishment)
Added damma to create base form
Added a tail (ى) at the end to make it mean 'first' for feminine things
Added ال (the) at the start to make it mean 'the first' instead of just 'first'
Added و with fatha to connect with previous word
This is the basic conditional particle that means 'if'

steps.m: indeed/truly (emphasizes فِى ذٰلِكَ لَعِبْرَةً - 'in that is a lesson')
steps.n: Adding the shadda changes this from a conditional 'if' to an emphasis word that makes what comes after it stronger. In this ayah, it emphasizes that there is definitely a lesson in what was mentioned before.
This is one of the most common prepositions in Arabic. It shows something is inside, within, or contained by something else.
Added the ى at the end which helps extend the 'ee' sound. In this ayah, it connects 'in' with 'that' (ذٰلِكَ) to mean 'in that'.
This is the basic demonstrative pronoun used to point to something nearby
The alif is replaced with a special mark (alif khanjariya) to show distance

steps.m: that (pointing to something far) (refers to the previous statement)
steps.n: ذٰ becomes ذٰلِكَ by adding لِكَ at the end. It's like adding a pointer that says 'look at what I just talked about'

steps.m: to learn from something by thinking about it deeply

steps.n: The root ع-ب-ر became a noun عِبْرَة (lesson) and لَ was added at the start to mean 'surely/definitely'. This connects to إِنَّ (indeed) at the start of the phrase to emphasize the lesson.

steps.m: surely a lesson (object of إِنَّ فِى ذٰلِكَ - indeed in that is)
steps.n: The word lost 'la' (لَ) at the start and gained tanween (-an) at the end to show it's the object being talked about
Combined two particles: لِ (for/to) and مَن (whoever/one who). This combination introduces the person who will receive or experience something.
Added shadda on ل to emphasize the meaning. This emphasizes that this lesson/sign is specifically and definitely for those who fear Allah.

steps.m: to fear or be in awe of something

steps.n: Adding vowels to show this is a past tense verb about feeling fear
Added يَ at the start to show present tense. The final ي changes to ى because it's at the end of a present tense verb.
Added ى at the end to show it's a special type of verb that ends in ى
Added تُمْ at the end to change 'you' to 'you all'
Added ءَ at the beginning to turn this into a question, like adding 'are' and '?' in English
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb
Changed the verb into an adjective by adding ي between second and third root letters
Added أَ at the beginning to make it comparative. The middle letter gets a shadda and damma to show it's comparing with something else. In this ayah, it's comparing with خَلْقًا (creation).

steps.m: to create or form something from nothing
steps.n: This is the basic meaning of the root
Changed the verb into a noun meaning 'creation'. This form makes the action into a thing we can talk about.
Added ًا to show this is the object being compared in strength. In the ayah, it's asking 'Are you stronger in creation (خَلْقًا) or is the heaven that He built?'

This is a special word that connects two parts of a question. Here it connects 'Are you...' with 'or is it the heaven...' to ask which is harder to create. The ِ at the end shows it connects to what comes next.

steps.m: to rise up or be elevated
steps.n: This is the basic meaning of the root
Connected the root letters س-م-و with vowels to make 'he/it rose up' in the past
Added ء at the end to make it a noun meaning 'sky' or 'heaven'
Added ٱل at the beginning to make it specific (like 'the' in English) and doubled the س with shadda
Added ُ at the end to show this is the subject of the sentence, and lengthened the ا to آ
This is the basic meaning of the root

steps.m: he built (referring to Allah building the sky)
steps.n: Added fathas to make it past tense. The ي changes to ى because it's at the end of the word. This word refers to Allah's act of building the sky, as mentioned in the ayah.

steps.m: he built (referring to Allah building the sky)
steps.n: Added alif maksura (ىٰ) to show the long vowel sound more clearly

steps.m: he built it (referring to Allah building the sky)
steps.n: Added هَا ('ha') at the end to mean 'it', referring back to the sky that Allah built

steps.m: to lift up or raise something higher

steps.n: Added fathas to connect the letters and show this happened in the past. This word is connected to فَسَوَّىٰهَا (then He made it proportionate) in the ayah, showing Allah did both actions.

steps.m: to raise up or make high

steps.n: Changed the verb idea into a concrete noun by adding vowel marks. This word refers to the height or elevation of the sky that Allah raised.
Added هَا at the end to show this refers to the sky's height. This word is what was raised (the object) in the phrase رَفَعَ سَمْكَهَا (He raised its height).
The shadda doubles the middle letter to show doing something thoroughly. The alif at the end (ىٰ) shows this is a complete action.

steps.m: and he made perfect (connected to رَفَعَ)
steps.n: Added فَ to show this action follows after رَفَعَ (he raised) in the previous word

steps.m: and He made it perfect (referring to سَمْكَهَا - its height)
steps.n: Added هَا to show what was made perfect - referring back to سَمْكَهَا (its height) mentioned earlier
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make it a past tense verb
Adding أَ at the start makes the verb causative - showing Allah is causing the darkness to happen to the night

steps.m: and He made dark (acting on لَيْلَهَا)
steps.n: Adding وَ connects this action to the previous statement in the verse
Connected the letters together and added vowel marks to make it a proper word
Added fatha at the end to show this word is being acted upon by the verb أَغْطَشَ (He darkened)

steps.m: its night (object of أَغْطَشَ - He darkened)
steps.n: Added هَا at the end which means 'its/her', referring back to something feminine (in this case, the earth or sky)
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Adding أ makes it causative - instead of just going out, it means to make something go out. The word ضُحَىٰهَا (its morning brightness) is what is being brought out.

steps.m: and He brought out (acted on ضُحَىٰهَا)
steps.n: Adding و connects this action with the previous statement وَأَغْطَشَ لَيْلَهَا (and He made its night dark)
Added ُ and َ marks to show it's a noun meaning the bright part of the morning. The last letter و changes to ى because it's at the end.

steps.m: its morning brightness (object of أَخْرَجَ - He brought forth)
steps.n: Added ها at the end to mean 'its' (referring to something feminine). This word is being acted upon by the verb أَخْرَجَ (He brought forth).
Added vowel marks to connect the letters together
Adding ال makes it refer to the specific earth we know

steps.m: and the earth (object of دَحَىٰهَآ/spread it out)
steps.n: Added وَ (and) at the start and َ at the end to show it's the object of the verb دَحَىٰهَآ (spread it out) in the ayah

steps.m: after (connects to دَحَىٰهَآ - He spread it)
steps.n: Changed into a word meaning 'after' by adding vowel marks. In this ayah, it tells when Allah spread out the earth - after what was mentioned before.
This is the basic demonstrative pronoun meaning 'this' for something close by
The alif is written with a special mark (ٰ) to show this points to something far away instead of close by
Adding ل with kasra (لِ) strengthens the meaning of distance
The كَ at the end makes it more specific, like saying 'that one' instead of just 'that'

steps.m: to spread out or extend something flat

steps.n: The root letters got vowels to make it past tense 'he did it'. The last letter و changed to ا which often happens at the end of Arabic words.
The ا changes to ى with a small alif (ٰ) above it. This is just a spelling change that doesn't affect the meaning.
Added هَا at the end to show what was spread out (referring to the Earth mentioned earlier in the verse)
Changed the ending to include an elongated alif (آ) which is just a pronunciation variation
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb

steps.m: he brought forth (مَآءَهَا وَمَرْعَىٰهَا - its water and its pasture)
steps.n: Adding أَ at the start and changing the vowels makes the verb causative - instead of just 'going out', it means 'making something go out'. This verb takes 'its water and its pasture' as the things being brought forth.
This is a combination of the preposition 'from' (مِنْ) and the feminine pronoun 'it/her' (ها)
Adding the fatha vowel mark makes this an active noun
Adding the long alif makes the word stronger/more emphatic

steps.m: its water (refers to the earth mentioned earlier)
steps.n: Added هَا at the end to show the water belongs to something (in this case, the earth)
Changes verb 'to graze' into the noun form meaning 'grazing'
Added مَ at the beginning to show it's a place where something happens (like a pasture), and changed the ending to ى
Added هَا to show it belongs to the earth (which is feminine in Arabic)
Added وَ meaning 'and' to connect it with the previous word مَآءَهَا (its water). Both are objects of the verb أَخْرَجَ (He brought forth)
Connected the root letters with fatha to form the basic noun
Changed first vowel from fatha to kasra to show many mountains
Added the definite article 'the'
Added 'and' at the beginning. The alif in ال becomes ٱ after وَ

steps.m: and the mountains (object of أَرْسَىٰهَا - He fixed them)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end to show this is the object being acted upon by the verb أَرْسَىٰهَا (He fixed/made firm)
Added vowels to connect the root letters. The و changes to ا at the end because it's a weak letter.
Added أَ at the beginning to show making something happen. The ا at the end changes to ى.

steps.m: He made it (the mountains) firmly fixed (connected to وَٱلْجِبَالَ)
steps.n: Added هَا which refers back to the mountains (الْجِبَالَ) mentioned just before this word. The ى gets an alif above it (ىٰ) to show it's a long vowel.

steps.m: to provide temporary benefit or enjoyment

steps.n: Added vowel marks and alif to make it into a noun meaning something that provides temporary benefit. This word describes what Allah provides for people and their cattle in the ayah.

steps.m: as a provision/benefit (for لَّكُمْ وَلِأَنْعٰمِكُمْ - for you and your cattle)
steps.n: The alif is written as a long alif (ٰ) and tanween (ًا) is added to show this is indefinite and in the accusative case. This describes how or why Allah provides this - as a provision for people and their cattle.
Starting with the basic preposition ل which means 'for' or 'to'
Adding shadda for emphasis makes the meaning stronger

steps.m: surely for you all (connected to مَتٰعًا - enjoyment)
steps.n: Adding كُمْ shows this is directed to multiple people. This word connects to مَتٰعًا (enjoyment) in the ayah, showing that the enjoyment is for you all.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to create the basic past tense verb form
From the verb 'to provide comfort', we get the plural noun meaning 'animals that provide comfort' (like cows, camels, sheep)
Adding the genitive case marker kasra since this word follows a preposition
Adding the preposition لِ which means 'for' or 'to'. The alif is lengthened to ٰ (alif khanjariya)
Adding the coordinating conjunction وَ (and) to connect with previous phrase
Adding the second person plural masculine possessive pronoun suffix كُمْ to show ownership

steps.m: when (introduces the time of جَآءَتِ - it came)
steps.n: This is a time word that shows when something happens. Here it introduces when the overwhelming event comes.

steps.m: then when/so when (introduces the time of جَآءَتِ - it came)
steps.n: The فَ at the beginning shows this happens after something mentioned before. It connects this event to what came earlier.
The middle letter ي changed to ا (alif) and got marks to make 'he came'
Added a longer alif to emphasize the 'coming'
Added تِ at the end to show the subject is feminine. This word connects to الطَّآمَّةُ (the overwhelming event) which is the subject doing the action.

steps.m: to overwhelm completely, to surpass all limits

steps.n: The middle letter has a shaddah (doubled), making it a past tense verb meaning 'it overwhelmed'. This word is actually the verb, not the subject, in relation to الطامة (the overwhelming event).
Adding alif after first letter changes it to active participle (something that overwhelms)
Adding ة makes it feminine and turns it into a noun meaning 'the overwhelming event'
The ٱل makes it 'the' overwhelming calamity, and the ُ ending shows it's doing the action of 'coming'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
Changed to feminine superlative form by adding damma, sukun and alif maqsura

steps.m: the greatest (referring to الطَّآمَّةُ - the overwhelming event)
steps.n: Added ال to make it specific and an alif to extend the final ى. This word describes the Day of Judgment as the greatest overwhelming event.
Connected the root letters together with vowel marks. This word tells us when the remembering (يَتَذَكَّرُ) happens.

steps.m: on the day when (object of يَتَذَكَّرُ)
steps.n: The fatha at the end shows this word is being used as a time expression, telling us when the remembering happens.
Connected the root letters with fatha to make basic past tense verb
Adding shadda doubles the middle root letter to create intensive meaning
Adding تَ makes the action reflexive - done to oneself
يَ at start shows present/future tense, ُ shows this is the doer of the action. This verb describes what happens on that day (يَوْمَ)
Forms the noun 'human' from the root - humans are beings who are naturally social and familiar with each other
ٱلْ makes it specific ('the human'), ُ shows this human is doing the remembering in يَتَذَكَّرُ (remembers)
This is a relative pronoun that connects to the verb سَعَىٰ (strived/did), showing what someone did

steps.m: he moved quickly with effort (past tense)
steps.n: Added fatha (َ) after each letter to make the basic past tense form

steps.m: he moved quickly with effort (what ٱلْإِنسٰنُ/the human did)
steps.n: The final ي with َ (ya with fatha) changes to ىٰ (alif maqsurah with superscript alif) because it's at the end of a word
Connected the root letters with fatha to make a basic past tense verb
Doubling the ر makes the meaning more intense and shows someone caused the action
Changed vowels to passive pattern to show the action happened to something
Added feminine marker ت and kasra ending because the subject (الجحيم) is grammatically feminine

steps.m: and was made to appear (feminine) (subject: الجحيم)
steps.n: Added و at start to connect this to what came before. This word describes how Hell (الجحيم) will be made clearly visible.
Connected the root letters with fatha to form the basic past tense verb
Added ي between second and third root letters to create a noun showing intense quality

steps.m: the hellfire (the specific one mentioned in the Quran)
steps.n: Added ال to make it definite, referring to the specific hellfire mentioned in the Quran

steps.m: the hellfire (subject of وَبُرِّزَتِ - 'and is made apparent')
steps.n: Added damma to show this is the subject of the verb 'made apparent' in the ayah
This is a question word that means 'whoever' or 'who'. In this ayah, it refers to the person who sees (يَرَىٰ)
Added لِ at the beginning to show purpose or direction - 'for' or 'to'

steps.m: to see with eyes or mind

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense verb
Added يَ at start and changed ending to ى to make it present tense 'he sees'
Changed to standard spelling of present tense 'to see'
Added alif khanjariyah (small alif) above the ى to guide pronunciation
This is a compound particle made up of multiple parts

steps.m: Then as for... (introduces condition about مَن طَغَىٰ)
steps.n: The فَ (fa) connects this condition with what came before it. This word introduces what will happen to 'whoever transgresses' (مَن طَغَىٰ)
This is a relative pronoun that refers to people. In this ayah, it refers to the person who transgressed (طَغَىٰ means 'transgressed/exceeded bounds')
Connected the letters together with fatha marks to show this happened in the past
The ى at the end shows this is about someone going beyond proper limits
Added small alif (ٰ) above the final letter to show the long 'aa' sound. This word describes what the person (مَن) in the ayah did.

steps.m: to prefer or choose something over another

steps.n: Added vowels (fatha) to make it past tense 'he preferred'
Added alif after first letter to show intensity of preference

steps.m: and he strongly preferred (الْحَيَوٰةَ الدُّنْيَا - the worldly life)
steps.n: Added وَ (and) to connect this action with previous statements. This word is followed by الْحَيَوٰةَ الدُّنْيَا (the worldly life) which is what was preferred.
Added fathas to connect the letters and changed the final ي to ا
Added و and ة to make it into a specific instance of the concept
Added ال to make it specific and َ at the end to show it's being acted upon by the verb 'preferred'
Root د-ن-و became دُنْيا (dunya) meaning 'worldly/lower' - a common word for 'this world'
Added ال to make it definite. In this ayah, it's part of الحَيَوٰةَ الدُّنْيَا (the worldly life) which is the object of آثَرَ (he preferred)

steps.m: indeed (emphasizes that الْجَحِيمَ is the destination)
steps.n: This is a particle that emphasizes what comes after it. Here it emphasizes that Hell is definitely the destination.

steps.m: then indeed (connects the consequence of الْجَحِيمَ being the destination)
steps.n: The فَ prefix shows this is a result or consequence of previous actions mentioned
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic past tense verb
Changes from a verb about burning to a noun meaning a super hot fire by adding 'ee' sound in middle
Makes the noun definite. This word is the object of فَإِنَّ (indeed/then) in this ayah.
Added the accusative case ending َ (fatha) because it's the object of فَإِنَّ (indeed/then)
This is the base part of the pronoun that points to something
Adding ى makes it specifically refer to feminine things or concepts
Adding َ (fatha) shows this is the subject doing something. In this ayah, it refers to الْجَحِيمَ (the Hellfire) which is الْمَأْوَىٰ (the refuge/dwelling place)
The base form of the verb means to give shelter to someone
Added مَ at the beginning to mean 'place of' and ى at the end because the word ends in a weak letter

steps.m: the place of shelter/refuge (predicate of هِىَ)
steps.n: Added ال to make it definite. This word completes the meaning of هِىَ (it is) in the ayah

steps.m: to lead to, to direct towards
steps.n: This starts from the basic particle أم which indicates direction or focus
Adding ا at the end changes this into a particle that introduces a new topic or contrast

steps.m: and as for (introduces the person who fears their Lord)
steps.n: The و connects this to the previous statement, showing a contrast or new topic. This introduces the person mentioned in the rest of the ayah who fears their Lord.

This is a relative pronoun that introduces a conditional clause. Here it refers to the person who fears their standing before their Lord. It connects directly to the verb خَافَ (feared) that comes after it.
Added sukun (ْ) on the ن to show the word ends here. This is a pronunciation helper that doesn't change the meaning.
Added short vowels (fatha) to make it a past tense verb meaning 'he feared'
The middle letter و changes to ا because it's a weak letter. This word is connected to وَنَهَى (and he forbade) in this ayah, showing two actions he did.

steps.m: place of standing (before رَبِّهِ - his Lord)
steps.n: Added مَ at the beginning and ا after ق to create a word meaning 'place where something happens'. This word is connected to رَبِّهِ (his Lord) in this ayah, showing whose presence is being stood in.

steps.m: to care for, nurture, or guide to completion

steps.n: The doubled middle letter (shown by ّ) makes this word mean 'the one who does the nurturing' rather than just the action of nurturing
The ِ shows this word belongs to or is connected to مَقَامَ (standing/position)
The هِ shows this belongs to 'him' (referring back to مَن - whoever)

steps.m: he stopped/prevented (subject of خَافَ - he feared)
steps.n: Added fathas to make it past tense. The ي at the end changes to ى because it's a special type of word. This word connects to خَافَ (he feared) from earlier in the verse.
Added و at the beginning to connect this action with what came before it in the verse

steps.m: to breathe, be precious, or be a self

steps.n: Added vowel marks to create a noun meaning 'soul' or 'self'
Added 'the' at the beginning and doubled the ن letter
Added fatha at the end to show this word is being acted upon by the verb نَهَى (forbade)
This is a preposition that shows something is being moved away from or avoided
Added kasra at the end to connect it smoothly to the next word (الْهَوَىٰ/desires). In this ayah, it shows restraining oneself away from following desires.

steps.m: to desire strongly or follow one's whims

steps.n: Adding the vowel marks makes it a complete past tense verb

steps.m: the desire/passion (that the soul is being prevented from in نَهَى ٱلنَّفْسَ عَنِ)
steps.n: Adding ال makes it refer to the specific concept of worldly desires that the soul is being prevented from in this context
Adding the superscript alif (ٰ) is just a writing convention that doesn't change the meaning
This is a particle that emphasizes what comes after it - in this case it's emphasizing that Paradise (ٱلْجَنَّةَ) is the refuge
The فَ shows this is a result or consequence of something mentioned before - it connects this statement about Paradise to what was said earlier
Connected the root letters and added a shadda to show emphasis on covering

steps.m: garden (a place that is covered/concealed by trees)
steps.n: Added ة to make it a noun referring to a place that is covered by trees and plants, which is why a garden is called جَنَّة in Arabic
Added ال to make it specific, referring to Paradise in particular
Added َ (fatha) at the end to show this is the object of فَإِنَّ (then indeed). Also changed اَلْ to ٱلْ as is standard in the Quran.
This is a basic pronoun used to refer to feminine things or concepts. In this ayah, it refers back to Paradise (الْجَنَّةَ) which is feminine in Arabic.
The root gives the idea of seeking shelter or refuge
Added مَ at the beginning to show 'place where' and ى at the end because the word ends in a weak letter

steps.m: the place of refuge (in relation to الْجَنَّةَ - Paradise)
steps.n: Added ال to make it specific. This word is describing Paradise (الْجَنَّةَ) in this verse.
Added the small alif (ٰ) as a pronunciation guide
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make it a past tense verb
Adding يَ at the start and changing vowels shows this is happening now or in the future
Adding ون shows multiple people are doing the action

steps.m: they ask you (about ٱلسَّاعَةِ - the Hour)
steps.n: Adding كَ shows who is being asked. This word connects to عَنِ ٱلسَّاعَةِ (about the Hour) which tells us what they're asking about.
This is the basic form of the preposition that shows what something is about or concerning. Here it connects the verb يَسْـَٔلُونَكَ (they ask you) to its topic ٱلسَّاعَةِ (the Hour).
Added kasra (ِ) at the end because the next word starts with ال. This makes it easier to pronounce.

steps.m: to measure time or move through time

steps.n: Root س-و-ع became سَاعَة by adding ة at the end to mean 'one unit of time'
Adding ال makes it specific/definite - 'the' hour instead of 'an' hour
Adding kasra at the end shows this word is being described by another word
This is the base question word meaning 'which'
Adding ان changes the meaning from 'which' to specifically asking about time

steps.m: when (asking about the Hour/Day of Judgment)
steps.n: In this ayah, it's specifically asking about the timing of the Day of Judgment (السَّاعَةِ mentioned in the previous words)
Added vowels to connect the root letters and make a past tense verb
Added مُ at start to show place/time, and changed end to ى because it's a place noun from a root ending in و
Added alif maksura marker to show the long vowel sound

steps.m: its appointed time (referring to the Hour/Day of Judgment)
steps.n: Added هَا to show it belongs to or refers to something feminine (the Hour/Day of Judgment mentioned earlier in the verse)
Starting with the preposition في which means 'in' or 'about'

steps.m: about what? (asking about something mentioned in أَنتَ مِن ذِكْرَىٰهَآ)
steps.n: Added the shortened question word 'ما' (what) as 'مَ' at the end. This combination creates an interrogative meaning. This word is asking about something mentioned in the following phrase 'you are from its mention/remembrance'
This is the basic form of the word meaning 'you' - it's a pronoun that doesn't come from a root

steps.m: you (when speaking to one male person)
steps.n: These are actually two different words - أَنْ means 'that' while أَنتَ is the separate word for 'you'. They are not related by transformation.
This is a basic preposition that shows where something starts or what something is about. In this ayah, it's asking about the reminder/hour.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make it a past tense verb
Changes from 'to remember' into 'a reminder' - like changing 'run' to 'a running'
Added هَآ which means 'her/its'. The word follows مِن (from/about) in the ayah.

steps.m: to (connects رَبِّكَ 'your Lord' as the destination)
steps.n: This is a preposition that shows movement or direction towards something. In this ayah, it connects to رَبِّكَ to show that 'your Lord' is the endpoint or destination being referred to.

steps.m: to (connects رَبِّكَ 'your Lord' as the destination)
steps.n: The alif at the end is written with a small mark (ىٰ) to show it's pronounced like a regular alif. This doesn't change the meaning.
Added basic vowels to connect the letters together
Changed from verb 'nurture' to noun 'lord' and added kasra at end to show something owns/possesses it
The كَ at the end shows this is 'your lord'. This word follows إِلَىٰ (to), showing direction or destination.

steps.m: to end or reach a limit
steps.n: The basic idea is about reaching a final point or conclusion
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb
Added مُ at start and ت between letters to make a noun showing where or how something ends
Added small alif (ٰ) to show this is a feminine word
Added هَآ at the end to show this endpoint belongs to something (in this case, it belongs to رَبِّ - your Lord)
Adding shadda makes the meaning stronger and more certain
Adding ما changes the meaning from 'indeed' to 'only'. This word restricts the meaning of the phrase after it (you are only a warner)
The alif is written with a special mark (آ) when it comes at the end of a word, but this doesn't change the meaning
This is the basic part of the pronoun that means 'you'

steps.m: you (when speaking to one male) (subject of مُنذِرُ)
steps.n: Added تَ to specify this is for speaking to one male person. This word is the subject of مُنذِرُ (warner) in this ayah, showing who is doing the warning.

steps.m: to warn or alert of danger

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
Adding مُ at the start and changing the middle vowel to kasra turns this into 'one who warns'. This describes 'you' (أَنتَ) in this ayah.
Adding damma at the end shows this is the main subject of the sentence, matching with أَنتَ (you)

steps.m: whoever (the subject of يَخْشَىٰهَا - fears it)
steps.n: This is a relative pronoun that connects two parts of a sentence. Here it means 'whoever' or 'the one who' and refers to people who fear. It's connected to the verb يَخْشَىٰهَا (fears it) that comes after it.
This is the basic concept of fearing something
Adding the vowels changes it from concept to past tense verb
Added يَ at the beginning to show this is happening now. The ي at the end changed to ى because it's a special type of word (weak letter word)
Added هَا at the end to show what is being feared (something feminine or abstract)
Basic comparison particle that shows similarity
Added أَنَّ to strengthen the comparison and make it hypothetical

steps.m: as if they (referring to the disbelievers on the Day of Judgment)
steps.n: Added هُمْ to show who is being referred to. This connects to the next ayah which describes what 'they' will experience on the Day of Judgment.
Connected the letters together and added vowel marks to make the basic noun form
Added fatha at the end because this word tells when the action يَرَوْنَ (they see) happens

steps.m: to see/perceive with eyes or mind

steps.n: Added يَ at beginning to show present tense. The hamza (أ) drops out in present tense due to weak letter rules.
Added وْنَ at the end to show multiple people (they) are doing the action

steps.m: they see it (referring to the Hour/Day of Judgment)
steps.n: Added هَا at the end which means "it" referring to the Hour/Day of Judgment mentioned in the context

steps.m: did not (changes يَلْبَثُوٓا۟ from 'remain' to 'did not remain')
steps.n: This is a negative particle that makes the verb after it negative and changes its time to the past. It's like adding 'did not' in English.
Connected the letters together with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
Added يَ at the beginning and changed vowels to show this happens in the present/future

steps.m: they stay/remain (in relation to لَمْ which makes it past tense)
steps.n: Added وا at the end to show many people are doing this action. The word comes after لَمْ which makes it refer to the past even though it's in present form.
This is the base form of the exception particle that limits or restricts what came before it. In this ayah, it restricts how long they stayed.
The word إِلْ becomes إِلَّا by adding a shadda and alif at the end. This makes it a complete word meaning 'except/only'. Like saying 'just' or 'only' in English.
Adding vowels to make it a past tense verb
Adding doubled ي changes it from a verb to a noun meaning 'evening'
Added ة to make it refer to one specific evening
Added tanween to show this is the object being acted upon in the sentence
This is a connecting word that gives a choice between two options. In this ayah, it connects 'evening' (عَشِيَّةً) with 'morning' (ضُحَىٰهَا) to show they stayed only for one of these short periods.
Changes from verb to noun meaning the bright morning time
The و changes to ىٰ (alif maqsurah) at the end of the word due to Arabic spelling rules

steps.m: its morning brightness (referring to the Day of Judgment)
steps.n: Added هَا to show this refers to the morning brightness of that specific day mentioned in the ayah
